sections.text: The ballad begins with lines from the hero's poetic self-praise (lines 1-6.3). Then his two wives are named and described (7-15). The action begins at night, when the junior wife rouses the hero from a dream and he reprimands her for it (lines 16-26). Having dreamed of marrying a beautiful woman at the foot of a mountain, he orders his wives to prepare his journey, and they somewhat truculently comply (lines 27-36). Properly equipped, the hero sets out and travels until he nears the king's palace (lines 37-52). He inquires about beer and is directed to the palace, where he greets the king and is given a full measure of beer (lines 53-71).

He resumes his journey, eventually coming to the foot of a mountain, where he meets two noblewomen gathering straw (lines 72-81). They greet, and the hero immediately proposes to them both; the one named Nyakaandalo refuses, citing the custom that forbids marrying two women in a single day (lines 82-95). The hero proposes to Nyakaandalo, but before she will answer, she asks him to identify himself; he does so by reciting his self-praise poetry and she accepts (lines 96-104.2).

After only six days of married life, the royal drums summon the warrior to battle; the hero must go (lines 105-18). He orders her to prepare provisions for his journey, but all the signs in this connection foretell disaster; he thinks she is trying to prevent his going (lines 119-36.1). He outfits himself, however poorly (lines 140-141.1). He departs, first reciting poetry he calls "[his own self-] praise" but is actually praise for their love and a description of an omen that may foretell his death (lines 144-50).

In the fields near Lugongo village, Kachwenyanja finds the enemy in a defensive posture. One of their number who wanted to form a blood brotherhood with the hero greets peacefully, but the hero throws the words back as a taunt, provoking battle (lines 151-58).<a name="line 159"> He kills four warriors, publically boasting and then sitting on the last in an act of self-display (lines 159-64).

A poorly dressed and equipped man appears on the enemy side and warns the hero to flee his bow, but the hero refuses (lines 164.1-73). Struck by the man's arrow, a weakened Kachwenyanja still boasts victory, but is cut down and mutilated by the enemy (174-84.1).

At home the wife and her maidservant prepare for his return (lines 186-90). Going to receive him, the wife asks about her husband from several warriors returning from the battle, but is not told the truth (lines 191-99.1). Finally, a warrior from another kingdom, who discerns the hero's identity through the wife's quoting of his self-praise, reveals the fact of the hero's death (lines 200-17).

Nyakandaalo laments (218-31). With the assistance of the maidservant, she decides on a course of revenge (lines 231.1-40). She promises vengeance in the form of poetic boast or self-praise (lines 241-43.1). She sets out and pauses at the hero's corpse to perform a ritual (244-48.1).

In enemy territory, she meets a series of men who would like to marry her. She asks each to identify himself with his self-praise and then rejects him on the putative basis of a totemic incompatibility he has revealed (lines 249-68). Finally, she meets and is proposed to by a poorly dressed man whose praise reveals him to be the killer of Kachwenyanja; she agrees to marry him (lines 269-87).

The couple lives as newly wed lovers for five days, after which the heroine thinks on revenge (lines 288-97). The heroine makes sure her knife is sharp and waits for him to come home drunk (lines 298-303). She plies him with womanly charms, and when he falls asleep, she cuts his throat (lines 304-12.1). <a name="line 313">The next day Nyakandaalo chides her latest husband's brothers and sister about his absence (lines 313-22). A brother comes to her that night, and she dispatches him with the same method (lines 323-31.1). The next day Nyakandaalo chides the other brothers, who proffer excuses on the husband's behalf (lines 332-43). In the evening, the two brothers visit, along with the sister-in-law; the heroine and her maidservant kill the brothers with the same method and then fall upon the sister-in-law and kill her also (lines 344-53).

Nyakandaalo and her maidservant remove the hero's belongings from the house and return to the place of the hero's death; the heroine's poetic utterance there combines self-praise for herself and lament for the hero (lines 354-85.1).
